elements.sent-2411-001: Superconducting circuit quantisation conventionally starts from classical Euler-Lagrange circuit equations-of-motion.
elements.sent-2411-002: Invoking the correspondence principle yields a canonically quantised circuit description of circuit dynamics over a bosonic Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-003: This process has been very successful for describing experiments, but implicitly starts from the classical Ginsberg-Landau (GL) mean field theory for the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-004: Here we employ a different approach which starts from a microscopic fermionic Hamiltonian for interacting electrons, whose ground space is described by the Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) many-body wavefuction that underpins conventional superconductivity.
elements.sent-2411-005: We introduce the BCS ground-space as a subspace of the full fermionic Hilbert space, and show that projecting the electronic Hamiltonian onto this subspace yields the standard Hamiltonian terms for Josephson junctions, capacitors and inductors, from which standard quantised circuit models follow.
elements.sent-2411-006: Importantly, this approach does not assume a spontaneously broken symmetry, which is important for quantised circuits that support superpositions of phases, and the phase-charge canonical commutation relations are derived from the underlying fermionic commutation properties, rather than imposed.
elements.sent-2411-007: By expanding the projective subspace, this approach can be extended to describe phenomena outside the BCS ground space, including quasiparticle excitations.
elements.sent-2411-008: Superconducting devices are one of the leading candidates for building quantum computers, showing great flexibility for implementing qubits and gates \cite{ref:SCcircuit_QI_outlook, ref:SCqubits_wilhelm .}
elements.sent-2411-009: The low working temperature reduces the noise, providing for long coherence time \cite{ref:SC_coherent .}
elements.sent-2411-010: The strong coupling between superconducting qubits and electromagnetic fields allows us to control and operate the system \cite{ref:1Dtransmon_control .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The conventional approach to quantising superconducting circuits \cite{ref:decoherence_SCqubits, ref:circuit_quantization starts with the classical circuit equations of motion, which can be derived from the standard Kirchoff's laws, which are derived from a combination of conservation laws and the constitutive relations for specific elements that are present in the circuit.}
elements.sent-2411-012: As long as the circuit is conservative, these equations are the Euler-Lagrange equations, which are generated from a Lagrangian.
elements.sent-2411-013: Canonical quantisation then proceeds, via the correspondence principle, by postulating commutation relations between classically conjugate coordinates describing the circuit phase space.
elements.sent-2411-014: These classical equations describe the dynamics of the Ginsburg-Landau (GL) superconducting order parameters, $\phi_j$, defined at the nodes $j$ of the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-015: The GL order parameter is itself a classical mean field, approximating an underlying microscopic electronic Hamiltonian, and the (re-)quantisation procedure of the classical Euler-Lagrange equations for $\phi_j$, described above, yields the standard bosonic theory for $\hat{\phi _j$ with the conjugate coordinates being the circuit charges, $\hat{n _j$.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: This standard approach (vertical block sequence in \cref{fig:flowchart ) is practically satisfactory for modelling the vast majority of experiments, however it is conceptually unfulfilling for several reasons.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Firstly, we already have a very successful microscopic quantum theory of superconductivity, namely, Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) theory, which describes the dynamics of the microscopic electronic Hilbert space of interacting electrons in a metal.
elements.sent-2411-018: As such, there should be a ``direct'' pathway from the microscopic electronic Hamiltonian to the circuit Hamiltonian, without leaving the fermionic Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-019: In this goal, there is close analogy in modelling low-dimensional semiconducting systems (such as quantum dots \cite{ref:quantumDot_Milburn or quantum Hall fluids \cite{ref:mesoscopic_QO, ref:mesoscopicChannel_QHE ), in which a microscopic Fermi-liquid theory of semiconductors is projected onto a low-dimensional subspace of low-energy bound states in a spatially inhomogeonous, weakly-confining potential landscape, yielding effective descriptions in terms of mass-renormalised, few-electron (or hole) systems \cite{ref:mesoscopic_QO .}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Secondly, the GL mean-field does not include phenomena such as quasiparticles or quantum fluctuations in the gap parameter, $\Delta$, which are otherwise added `by-hand' after re-quantisation \cite{ref:Higgs_dynamics_Floquent, ref:quasiparticles_SCqubits .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Thirdly, when re-quantising GL mean-field theory (MFT), bosonic canonical commutation relations are simply postulated from the classically-conjugate coordinates; in fact, we expect the correct commutation relations should emerge from the underlying fermionic degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-022: Fourthly, GL mean-field theory assumes spontaneous symmetry breaking of the superconducting order parameter, however in the quantised circuit, we expect quantum superpositions of (relative) phases which is conceptually at odds with the notion of the spontaneous realisation of one (amongst the continuous manifold) of allowed phases.
elements.sent-2411-023: Lastly, the classical GL MFT for the order parameter $\phi$ is indifferent to the support of $\phi$: compact, $\phi\in [-\pi,\pi)$, and non-compact, $\phi\in\mathbb{R}$, descriptions of $\phi$ are entirely equivalent at a classical level.
elements.sent-2411-024: In contrast, the quantum descriptions of these two alternatives, or something in between, are not equivalent to one another \cite{ref:compact-noncompact .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Starting from the classical GL MFT therefore cannot resolve which alternative provides the more accurate description.
elements.sent-2411-026: The objective of this work is to provide a somewhat formal derivation of the Hamiltonian for superconducting circuits, starting from the microscopic theory of interacting electrons.
elements.sent-2411-027: To do this, we reformulate BCS theory slightly, by introducing a low energy Hilbert space of the of the set of BCS ground states, $\mathcal{H _{\rm BCS =\text{span}\{\ket{\Psi(\phi_j)}\}_{\phi_j\in \mathcal{Z}\subset[-\pi,\pi)}$, where $\ket{\Psi(\phi_j)}$ is a BCS ground state (defined later), parameterised by a BCS phase angle $\phi_j$.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: We define a projector, $P$ onto $\mathcal{H _{\rm BCS}$, and use this projector to construct the low energy Hamiltonian (refer to the outer arrow in the flowchart \cref{fig:flowchart ).}}
elements.sent-2411-029: We illustrate this approach for the conventional superconducting toolbox: capacitors, inductors, and Josephson junctions.
elements.sent-2411-030: In subsequent sections we formalise this approach, and derive a number features of the model, including the discrete basis for $\mathcal{H _{\text{BCS }$, the commutation relations in the compact $\mathcal{H _{\text{BCS }$, and support for the low-energy Hilbert space of standard superconducting elements (inductors vs Josephson junctions).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The new microscopic framework equips us with new insight of the matter phase and can be easily generalised to a bigger Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-032: With this approach, we develop the connection between superconducting BCS theory \cite{ref:BCS_superconductivity_theory and the well-known Cooper-pair-box (CPB) model \cite{ref:CPB .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: This paper is organised as below: We firstly carry on the overlap of BCS states and discuss the requirements of discrete basis set constructed by them in Section~\ref{sec:LowEspace .}
elements.sent-2411-034: In Section~\ref{sec:LCJprojection , we project the LCJ circuit from the first principles and include the external field to the circuit.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Finally we conclude the work and discuss the future outlook in Section~\ref{sec:conclusion .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.7\columnwidth]{figures/flowchart.pdf} \caption{ The conventional approach to circuit quantisation (left pathway) conceptually begins with a microscopic electronic theory of interacting fermions in a metal, which proceeds to a classical Ginsburg-Landau mean-field theory (GL-MFT) of superconductivity in a metal, which is finally requantised to produce a quantised theory of a circuit. The approach described here (right pathway) starts at the same point, but uses the GL-MFT as inspiration to define a low-energy BCS subspace, $\mathcal{H}_{\rm BCS}$ (see \cref{eq:HBCS}) and then directly project the dynamics onto this subspace, resulting in the effective low-energy circuit Hamiltonian. } \label{fig:flowchart}
elements.sent-2411-036: To introduce notation, we consider two pieces of superconducting metal separating by an insulator, which is equivalently an LCJ circuit shown in \cref{fig:system .}
elements.sent-2411-037: For the Josephson junction illustrated in \cref{fig:system ~(b), the superconducting islands $A$ and $B$ are described by their respecitve Hamiltonians \cite{ref:annett , \begin{equation}\label{eq:Hamiltonian_SCwoMFA} H_{A,B} = H_{K_{A,B}}+H_{I_{A,B}},\end{equation} where the microscopic electron kinetic and interaction terms are given respectively in the momentum basis as \begin{equation}\label{eq:HKA} H_{K_A} = \sum_{k_A s_A} \left( \epsilon_{k_A} - \mu_A \right) c_{k_A s_A}^\dagger c_{k_A s_A}, H_{I_A} = - \abs{g}^2 \sum_{k_Ak_A'} c_{k_A\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-k_A\downarrow}^\dagger c_{-k_A'\downarrow} c_{k_A'\uparrow},\end{equation} and similarly for $H_B$.}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{eq:HKA} H_{K_A} = \sum_{k_A s_A} \left( \epsilon_{k_A} - \mu_A \right) c_{k_A s_A}^\dagger c_{k_A s_A}, H_{I_A} = - \abs{g}^2 \sum_{k_Ak_A'} c_{k_A\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-k_A\downarrow}^\dagger c_{-k_A'\downarrow} c_{k_A'\uparrow},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-038: Assuming point-like metallic islands, the Coulomb interaction between electrons on island $A$ and $B$ is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:Hamiltonian_coulomb} H_\text{Coul} = \lambda_C \sum_{\bm{k}_A \bm{k}_B s_A s_B} c_{\bm{k}_A s_A}^\dagger c_{\bm{k}_A s_A} c_{\bm{k}_B s_B}^\dagger c_{\bm{k}_B s_B},\end{equation} where $\lambda_C = \frac{1 {4\pi\epsilon \frac{Q_A Q_B {r^2}$ is the effective Coulomb interaction strength between the two point-like superconductor.}}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Hamiltonian_coulomb} H_Coul = \lambda_C \sum_{k_A k_B s_A s_B} c_{k_A s_A}^\dagger c_{k_A s_A} c_{k_B s_B}^\dagger c_{k_B s_B},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-039: The electrons also tunnel between through the insulating barrier, with tunneling Hamiltonian \begin{equation}\label{eq:Hamiltonian_tunneling} H_T = \displaystyle\sum_{\bm{k}_A \bm{k}_B s \sigma} t_{\bm{k}_{A}\bm{k}_{B}} c_{\bm{k}_A s}^{\dagger} c_{\bm{k}_B s} + t_{\bm{k}_A\bm{k}_B}^* c_{\bm{k}_B \sigma}^{\dagger} c_{\bm{k}_A \sigma}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Hamiltonian_tunneling} H_T = \displaystyle\sum_{k_A k_B s \sigma} t_{k_{A}k_{B}} c_{k_A s}^{\dagger} c_{k_B s} + t_{k_Ak_B}^* c_{k_B \sigma}^{\dagger} c_{k_A \sigma}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-040: The parameters and the operators in the Hamiltonians are $\epsilon_{\bm{k }$: the kinetic energy of the electron with momentum $\bm{k}$, $\mu$: the chemical potential, $g$: the effective coupling strength between electrons, $t_{\bm{k _A\bm{k _B}$: tunneling matrix element depending on the initial and final states $\bm{k _A$, $\bm{k _B$ respectively, and $c_{\bm{k _ls_l ^\dagger$ and $c_{\bm{k _ls_l}$: creation and annihilation operators for electron of momentum $\bm{k _l$ and spin $s_l$ (index $l$ labels the superconductor).}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The microscopic Hamiltonian for an electrical circuit including tunnel junctions is then \( H_{\text{LCJ = H_A + H_B + H_\text{Coul + H_T.}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The quadratic Hamiltonian is diagonalised by the Bogoliubov-Valatin transformation to construct the BCS ground state \begin{equation}\ket{\Psi(\Delta)} = \prod_{\bm{k}} \left( u_{\bm{k}}(|\Delta|) + v_{\bm{k}}(|\Delta|) e^{i\arg(\Delta)} c_{\bm{k}\uparrow}^{\dagger} c_{-\bm{k}\downarrow}^{\dagger} \right) \ket{0},\label{eq:BCSGS}\end{equation} which is used to self-consistently solve for $|\Delta|$, and for which the phase $\phi\equiv\arg(\Delta)$ is a free classical coordinate.
elements.sent-2411-043: We use this standard approach to motivate a choice of low energy basis states, but depart from it in that we do not impose the symmetry breaking implied by taking the partial expectations that transforms the microscopic quartic Hamiltonian into a quadratic form.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.49\columnwidth]{figures/LCJcircuit.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.49\columnwidth]{figures/system.pdf} \caption{ (a) A standard LCJ circuit consists of a inductor $L$, a capacitor $C$, and a Josephson junction $E_J$ made by superconductors. (b) A Josephson junction consists of two superconducting islands (blue) separated by an insulating tunnel barrier (yellow). At low temperature, the electrons bind into Cooper pairs which tunnel coherently across the (green arrow). In addition, the charges inside the two superconducting slabs have a capacitive Coulomb interaction (red arrow). } \label{fig:system}
elements.sent-2411-044: In this section, we use the well-known phase-symmetry-broken BCS ground state $\ket{\Psi(\Delta)}$ to motivate the introduction of a low-energy Hilbert space spanned by a set of equivalent BCS ground states $\{\ket{\Psi(\phi)}\}_{\phi\in[-\pi,\pi)}$.
elements.sent-2411-045: We analyse the overlap of two BCS states from this set, and determine conditions for approximate basis orthogonality and completeness.
elements.sent-2411-046: We then construct ground-space phase and number operators, and connect the resulting phase-number commutator to the the now-venerable Pegg-Barnett theory \cite{ref:Pegg Barnett .}
elements.sent-2411-047: A ground state wavefunction of many paired electrons inside a superconductor is the BCS state \cite{ref:BCS_superconductivity_theory \begin{equation}\label{eq:BCS_state} \ket{\Psi(\phi_j)} = \prod_{\bm{k}} \left( u_{\bm{k}} + v_{\bm{k}} e^{i\phi_j} c_{\bm{k}\uparrow}^{\dagger} c_{-\bm{k}\downarrow}^{\dagger} \right) \ket{0},\end{equation} where $\phi_j\in [-\pi,\pi)$ is the phase of the superconductor, $u_{\bm{k }$, $v_{\bm{k }$ are variational parameters used to minimised the total energy, and $\ket{0}$ is the vacuum for electron Fock state.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: We adopt the subscript $j$ to preempt our intent to restrict the allowed phases to a discrete subset $\mathcal{Z =\{\phi_j\}_{j\in \Zjmax}$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The core of our approach here is to define a basis of BCS ground states $\{\ket{\Psi(\phi_j)}\}_{\phi_j\in \mathcal{Z }$ and the corresponding low-energy Hilbert space \begin{equation}\mathcal{H}_{\rm BCS}=\text{span}\{\ket{\Psi(\phi_j)}\}_{\phi_j\in \mathcal{Z}}.\label{eq:HBCS}\end{equation} Projecting the microscopic electronic Hamiltonian into this subspace in which we (re)construct the low energy circuit theory without recourse to a re-quantisation procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-050: For a system consisting of multiple superconducting islands, the joint system forms a tensor product of the subsystems.
elements.sent-2411-052: This work is focussed on the dynamics of an electronic system restricted to the BCS ground space, so we define an (approximate) BCS ground space projection operator $P$ onto $\mathcal{H _{\text{BCS }$ \cite{ref:POM as \begin{equation}\label{eq:projector} P = \sum_j \dyad{\Psi(\phi_j)}, \quad \bar{P} = \mathbb{I} - P,\end{equation} where $\mathbb{I}$ denotes the identity operator.}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The degree to which this is a well defined projector satisfying $P^2=P$ depends on the orthogonality of the ground state basis, which we address below.
elements.sent-2411-054: We will use the projection operator to construct effective operators acting on the low energy space.
elements.sent-2411-055: For example, a system of two superconducting islands with a microscopic electronic Hamiltonian $H_{\rm el =H_A + H_B + H_\text{Coul +H_T$, the low-energy effective Hamiltonian is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:Heff} PH_{eff}P \nonumber \approx PH_0P + PH_1P \quad+ PH_1\bar{P} \left( \bar{P}E_0 - \bar{P}H_0\bar{P} \right)^{-1} \bar{P}H_1P,\end{equation} up to the second-order in the tunneling, where \mbox{$H_0 = H_A + H_B + H_\text{Coul}$} and $H_1 = H_T$ is treated perturbatively.}}

elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=.49\columnwidth]{figures/BCS_overlap.pdf} \includegraphics[width=.49\columnwidth]{figures/orthogonality_overlap.pdf} \caption{ (a) Overlap of the BCS states $\mathcal{W}(\varphi) = \ip{\Psi(\phi)}{\Psi(\phi')}$ as a function of the phase difference $\varphi \equiv \phi' - \phi$. For this plot we have set $b=10$. (b) The overlap amplitude of adjacent basis states \cref{eq:orthogonality} As $\alpha$ grows, neighbouring phase states become increasingly orthogonal. } \label{fig:BCS_overlap orthogonality}

elements.sent-2411-065: Nonetheless \cref{fig:BCS_overlap orthogonality a shows that $\mathcal{W (\varphi)$ is a rapidly decreasing function of $|\varphi|$, and so we construct an approximately orthogonal basis by discretising $\phi\in[-\pi,\pi)$ with discretisation interval $\delta\phi=\phi_{j+1 - \phi_j$, to construct the discrete set of phases $\phi_j=-\pi+\delta\phi\cdot j$ with \mbox{$ j\in\Zjmax\equiv\{0,1,...,\jmax=\lfloor 2\pi/\delta\phi\rfloor\}$}, so that \begin{equation}\phi_j\in \mathcal{Z}=2\pi(\Zjmax/\jmax-1/2)\subset[-\pi,\pi).\label{eq:Zset}\end{equation} From \cref{eq:BCSoverlap_approx , the overlap amplitude of neighbouring basis states is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:overlap_adjacentBCSstate} \abs{W(\delta\phi)} = e^{-\delta\phi^2 n \pi/(32 b)}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: We choose $\delta\phi={2\pi\alpha /{\sqrt{n }$ where $\alpha$ is a parameter controlling the overlap, so that \begin{equation}\label{eq:orthogonality} \abs{W(\delta\phi)} \approx e^{-\pi^3 \alpha^2/(8 b)}=e^{-\pi \alpha^2/(2 \alpha_0^2)}\end{equation} is independent of the electron number, and we define $\alpha_0=2 \sqrt{b /\pi$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: With the choice of $\delta\phi$ above, the dimension of the BCS subspace basis is \mbox{$\jmax=\sqrt{n /\alpha$}.}
elements.sent-2411-068: This has the effects that the phase states become increasingly dense in the interval $[-\pi,\pi)$ as $n$ grows, and that $\jmax\ll n$, so that the effective dimension of the BCS subspace is much smaller than the number of single-particle modes.

The parameters are chosen to be $n=10^3$, $b=10$ here. } \label{fig:projErr}
elements.sent-2411-088: Having constructed a formal low energy Hilbert space from a discrete basis of phase states, in this section we define a basis of ``number'' states through a Fourier transformation.
elements.sent-2411-089: These two bases implicitly define the low-energy phase and charge operators, $\hat\phi$ and $\hat N$, and from these we compute commutation relations.

elements.sent-2411-091: Here we define low-energy ``charge'' eigenstates $\ket{\Psi(N)}$ via a discrete Fourier transform \begin{equation}\label{eq:|N>_def} \ket{\Psi(N)} \equiv \left( \dfrac{\alpha}{\sqrt{n-1}+\alpha} \right)^{\sfrac{1}{2}} \sum_{\phi_j} e^{-iN\phi_j} \ket{\Psi(\phi_j)},\end{equation} where the prefactor ensures normalisation.

elements.subsection-2411-009: Inductance: Spatial BCS Theory and Electromagnetic Fields
elements.sent-2411-159: We have discussed the Coulomb interaction between metals and the electron tunneling inside the superconducting junction, which corresponds to the capacitance and the junction energy of the circuit, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-160: Here we are going to analyse the inductance part of the device.
elements.sent-2411-161: By applying a field to the system, the charge carriers will be accelerated, from which the kinetic inductance arises, and the electromagnetic energy will be transferred to the geometric inductance energy of the device.
elements.sent-2411-162: The complete manifestation is analysed in \cref{append:Afield_SCwire .}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/SC_straightWire.pdf} \caption{ (a) A cylindrically symmetric superconducting wire. The wire carries a supercurrent $i_s = \int_{\bm{\sigma}} \bm{J} \vdot \dd{\bm{\sigma}}$, which induces an internal field inside the superconductor (\cref{append:Afield_SCwire}). The external magnetic field penetrates a small depth $\lambda_L = \sqrt{m / (\mu_0 D e^2)}$ into the superconductor, where $D$ is the electron density in the material and $m$ is electron mass. (b) The 1D approximation model to (a). We consider the effective cross-section area \( \sigma_\text{eff} = \pi r_w^2 - \pi (r_w - \lambda_L)^2 \approx 2\pi r_w\lambda_L, \) i.e., the area that the applied vector potential can penetrate into. The supercurrent in the 1D model is the maximal value in the 2D model: $\bm{J} = J_z^\text{max} \bm{e}_z$. } \label{fig:SC_straightWire}
elements.sent-2411-163: Here we consider a superconducting wire with finite cross-section area shown in \cref{fig:SC_straightWire (a).}
elements.sent-2411-164: For simplicity, we will take the wire to be cylindrical, so that the problem is a 2D system (while realistic microelectronic wires are 3D).
elements.sent-2411-165: In the 2D case, the superconducting phase and the vector potential depend on both the axial and radial coordinates, $z$ and $r$.
elements.sent-2411-166: However we seek to describe the problem as a quasi-1D wire, shown in \cref{fig:SC_straightWire (b), characterised by an axially varying phase, $\phi(z)$.}
elements.sent-2411-167: Our goal is to derive the constitutive relation and the relevant Hilbert space for a lumped inductive element from the microscopic wavefunction of a distributed, spatially-varying phase in superconducting wire of length $l$.

elements.subsubsection-2411-008: Emergent Low-energy Hilbert Space of an Inductor
elements.sent-2411-205: While we have described a point-like superconducting element (e.g. a capacitor or tunnel junction) with a periodic coordinate $\phi\in[-\pi,\pi)$, which respects the translation symmetries of these devices, the inductive constitutive law \cref{eq:supercurrentOP_QM breaks translation symmetry, and the Hilbert space of $\hat \phi_{AB}$ is substantially larger.}
elements.sent-2411-206: Here we provide anphysically-motivated argument that bounds the physically relevant Hilbert space for an inductive element.
elements.sent-2411-207: As discussed above, the discretised 1D inductor is formed from $M$ segments, and recalling the definition of $\mathcal{Z}\in[-\pi,\pi)$, the natural Hilbert space for the wire is \begin{equation}\label{eq:emergent_space_range} H_wire = H_A \otimes \cdots \otimes H_B = Z^{\otimes M-1},\end{equation} where the exponent $M-1$ indicates that the relevant internal degrees of freedom are the relative phases \mbox{$\Delta\phi_j=\phi_{z_j+1 -\phi_{z_j}\in\mathcal{Z}\subset[-\pi,\pi)$} between neighbouring segments.}
elements.sent-2411-208: The subsequent ``lumped-element'' approximation treats dynamics of the interior segments as being determined by the boundary values $\phi_{A,B}$, and so the accessible states within $\mathcal{H _\text{wire $ satisfy $\Delta\phi_j =(\phi_{B -\phi_A)/M$ for each $j$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-209: It follows that $\phi_{B -\phi_A\in M[-\pi,\pi)$, which determines the effective, lumped element Hilbert space of the discretised wire.}
elements.sent-2411-210: We next note that $M$ is bounded by the physical constraint that the supercurrent must be below the critical current, $i_c$, of the wire.
elements.sent-2411-211: In particular, the total current in the wire is $\abs{i_s = \Phi_0 |\phi_B-\phi_A|/L\leq \Phi_0 M\pi/L<i_c$, and so \begin{equation}M \leq M_\text{max} = {i_c L}/{\Phi_0}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-212: This maximal number depends on the gap and the temperature because \( i_c = \frac{\Delta {\lambda_L(T) \sqrt{\frac{\rho_F {2\mu_0 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-213: We comment in passing that this argument really asserts that the dissipationless description of the inductive element requires the phase drop across the inductor to be bounded in the interval $\phi_{AB}\in M[-\pi,\pi)$.
elements.sent-2411-214: However, the discretisation imposed here is not physical, and so it is physically possible for $\phi_{AB}$ to exceed this range, at which point the current exceeds the critical value, and the system becomes dissipative via quasiparticle formation.
elements.sent-2411-215: A more formal treatment of this would require the inclusion of these states in a larger Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-216: This is beyond the goals of this work, and we leave this to future research.

elements.sent-2411-240: According to WKB method, the tunnel matrix element $t$ is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:tunnel_MtxElem} t = h f \cdot T = hf \cdot e^{-2l_I\sqrt{2m_e^* U_0}/\hbar},\end{equation} where $T$ is the tunneling probability, $l_I$ is the thickness of the insulator, and $m_e^*$ is the effective electron in the superconductor ($m_e^*=0.4m_e$ for aluminium oxide).
elements.formula-2411-080: \begin{equation}\label{eq:tunnel_MtxElem} t = h f \cdot T = hf \cdot e^{-2l_I\sqrt{2m_e^* U_0}/\hbar},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-241: The potential height $U_0$ is measured by the Fermi level, and depends on the thickness $l_I$ and the material \cite{ref:barrier-thickness_Al .}

elements.sent-2411-246: We describe the error in $T$ by the correction formula \begin{equation}T_\beta = e^{-2l_I\sqrt{2\beta m_e^* U_0}/\hbar},\end{equation} where $\beta$ denotes the possible error from the insulator thickness $l_I$, potential height $U_0$, and the effective mass $m_e^*$.

elements.sent-2411-271: We propose a fully-quantum approach to the quantisation of superconducting circuits in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-272: By restricting to the low-energy sector, we derive the effective Hamiltonian of the superconducting LCJ circuit from first principles.
elements.sent-2411-273: With the fully-quantum framework, we establish the connection between the microscopic and the macroscopic pictures.
elements.sent-2411-274: Taking a standard LCJ circuit for instance, (1) the Coulomb interaction between the two superconductors gives rise to the capacitance part; (2) each electron tunneling contributes an additional phase to the junction energy; (3) the electromagnetic field inside and outside the device relate to the inductance energies.
elements.sent-2411-275: From the energy point of view, the magnetic flux enters the circuit Hamiltonian as a phase shift, and contributes significantly only to the inductance term.
elements.sent-2411-276: During the derivation, we choose a discrete basis set for projection, and show that the required subspace dimension $\order{\sqrt{n }$ is much smaller than the total electron mode number $n$ of the system.}
elements.sent-2411-277: Moreover, we show that the subspace emerges, which is bounded by the critical current of the superconductor, as inductors are included to the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-278: An analytical form to the Higgs modes in superconductors is provided in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-279: With quartic and quadratic approximations to the energy spectrum, we obtain the order parameter at zero-temperature and the effective mass of the oscillator, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-280: Another highlight of the work is the ambiguity in compactness.
elements.sent-2411-281: Following the definition of the discrete basis set in phase domain, the matter phase $\phi$ has to be with a certain range, and therefore is compact.
elements.sent-2411-282: However, the inclusion of the external fields turns it into non-compact as the field is arbitrary.
elements.sent-2411-283: Nevertheless the discreteness and the non-compactness in phase have little affect to the canonical relation and the energy spectrum in practical.
elements.sent-2411-284: Our approach is easily generalised to multilevel systems and the time-dependence can be included in the external field as well.
elements.sent-2411-285: While the constant field has no effect to the capacitance energy, a time-varying field will enter in its time-derivative form.
elements.sent-2411-286: As the supercurrent determines the range of the phase operator, it breaks the BCS symmetry and therefore an open system has to be considered.
elements.sent-2411-287: By including the higher energy levels, who act as the dissipation channels, one can explore the contribution of the quasiparticles.
elements.sent-2411-288: In addition to the Josephson junction, more superconducting devices such as quantum phase slip (QPS) \cite{ref:2nlinearSCqubit can be explored by this approach, by simply substituting the device's Hamiltonian and compute the projection.. .}

elements.sent-2411-328: We then compute the Matsubara sum by a contour integral \cite{ref:bruus : \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\dfrac{1}{\beta} \sum_{iE_n} F_{\uparrow\downarrow} (k_A, iE_n) F_{\uparrow\downarrow}^* (k_B, i(E_n-E_0)) \nonumber = \dfrac{1}{\beta} \sum_{iE_n} \dfrac{-\Delta} {(iE_n)^2 - \left( (\epsilon_{k_A}-\mu_A)^2 + \abs{\Delta}^2 \right) } \dfrac{-\Delta} {(iE_n-iE_0)^2 - \left( (\epsilon_{k_B}-\mu_B)^2 + \abs{\Delta}^2 \right) }, \approx \dfrac{\Delta^2}{4E_{k_A}E_{k_B}} \Big( \dfrac{1}{iE_0 + E_{k_A} + E_{k_B}} - \dfrac{1}{iE_0 - E_{k_A} - E_{k_B}} \Big), \;\;for  \beta\rightarrow\infty, = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{2E_{k_A}E_{k_B}} \cdot \dfrac{E_{k_A} + E_{k_B}} {E_0^2 + (E_{k_A} + E_{k_B})^2}. \label{eq:Matsubara_sum}\end{equation}\end{subequations The residues occurs at the poles \( i\mathcal{E _r = \pm E_{\bm{k _A , iE_0\pm E_{\bm{k _B .}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-331: We evaluate the double integral $\mathcal{I (E_0, \Delta)$ in \cref{append:double_integral .}}
elements.sent-2411-332: In this appendix, we are going to compute $\mathcal{I (E_0,\Delta)$ in two limits: (1) $E_0 \ll \Delta$ and (2) $E_0 \gg \Delta$.}
elements.sent-2411-333: We expand the integral at $E_0=0$ for small $E_0$ case: \begin{equation}I \approx \int_\Delta^{\infty} \dd{E_{k_A}} \int_\Delta^{\infty} \dd{E_{k_B}} \Big( \dfrac{2}{ \left(E_{k_A} + E_{k_B}\right) \sqrt{ (E_{k_A}^2 - \Delta^2) (E_{k_B}^2 - \Delta^2) }} - \dfrac{2E_0^2}{ \left(E_{k_A} + E_{k_B}\right)^3 \sqrt{ (E_{k_A}^2 - \Delta^2) (E_{k_B}^2 - \Delta^2) }} \Big) + \order{(\sfrac{E_0}{\Delta})^4}, \nonumber = \dfrac{\pi^2}{2\Delta} - \dfrac{E_0^2\pi^2}{32\Delta^3} + \order{(\sfrac{E_0}{\Delta})^4}, \quad for E_0 \ll\Delta.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-334: The large $E_0$ case is described by the similar expansion around $E_0 \rightarrow \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-335: Introducing the triangle transformation in \cref{fig:triangle_transformation (a) with \mbox{$E_{\bm{k _A \equiv \Delta / \sin\theta_A$} and \mbox{$E_{\bm{k _B \equiv \Delta / \sin\theta_B$}, the integral changes: \( \int_\Delta^\infty \dd{E_{\bm{k _A \int_\Delta^\infty \dd{E_{\bm{k _B \rightarrow \int_0^{\pi/2 \dd{\theta_A (-\Delta\cot\theta_A\csc\theta_A) \int_0^{\pi/2 \dd{\theta_B (-\Delta\cot\theta_B\csc\theta_B).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.section-2411-010: Vector potential of a Superconducting Wire
elements.sent-2411-367: In this appendix, we solve the London equation for a superconducting wire in a cylindrical coordinates and obtain the magnetic field inside the wire.
elements.sent-2411-368: The system can be referred to \cref{fig:SC_straightWire .}
elements.sent-2411-369: We then estimate the flux around the wire out to a specific distance, and calculate the geometric inductance as the magnetic field is linked to the current.
elements.sent-2411-370: In addition, we also compute the $\bm{A ^\text{int}$-field inside the superconductor induced by the supercurrent.}
elements.sent-2411-371: This field associates to the change of the electromotive force inside the metal and gives rise to the kinetic inductance.
elements.sent-2411-372: With Ampére's law, the London equation is expressed as \( \curl{\curl{\bm{H ^\text{int + \bm{H ^\text{int / \lambda_L^2 = 0, \) where $\bm{B ^\text{int = \mu_0 \bm{H ^\text{int}$ and $\lambda_L$ is London penetration depth.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-373: Considering a circular superconducting wire with radius $r_w$, we have $\bm{H ^\text{int (r) = H_\theta(r) \bm{e _\theta$ according to the cylindrical symmetry.}}}
elements.sent-2411-374: The fields inside the metal must be finite and the boundary condition guarantees $\bm{H ^\text{int (r=r_w) = H_0 \bm{e _\theta$, so the solution reads \begin{equation}\label{eq:Hfield_SCwire} \bm{H}^\text{int} (r) = H_0 \dfrac{I_1 (r/\lambda_L)}{I_1 (r_w/\lambda_L)} \bm{e}_\theta,\end{equation} where $I_\alpha (z)$ is the modified Bessel function of the first kind, and $H_0$ is the magnetic field at the surface of the cylinder.}}}
elements.sent-2411-375: From Maxwell's equation (with zero electric field) $\curl{\bm{H ^\text{int = \bm{J}$, the supercurrent density is along the axial direction: \begin{equation}\label{eq:supercurrent_density} \bm{J} (r) = \dfrac{H_0}{\lambda_L} \dfrac{I_0 (r/\lambda_L)}{I_1 (r_w/\lambda_L)} \bm{e}_z = J_z(r) \bm{e}_z.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-376: The supercurrent $\bm{J}$ in \cref{eq:supercurrent_density is exponentially suppressed at the centre, but is not identically zero.}
elements.sent-2411-377: The maximal values is at the surface $\bm{J _{\text{max = \bm{J (r_w) = \frac{H_0 {\lambda_L}\bm{e _z$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-378: The total current flowing through the wire is the surface integral over the cross section, \begin{equation}\label{eq:supercurrent} i_s = \int_{\bm{\sigma}} \bm{J} \vdot \dd{\bm{\sigma}} = 2\pi \int_0^{r_w} dr \,r \, J_z(r) = 2\pi r_w H_0,\end{equation} such that the $\bm{B ^\text{ext}$-field outside the wire is given by \begin{equation}\bm{B}^\text{ext} (r) = \frac{\mu_0 i_s}{2\pi r} \bm{e}_\theta = \frac{\mu_0 r_w H_0}{r} \bm{e}_\theta.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-142: \begin{equation}\label{eq:supercurrent} i_s = \int_{\sigma} J \vdot \dd{\sigma} = 2\pi \int_0^{r_w} dr \,r \, J_z(r) = 2\pi r_w H_0,\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-143: \begin{equation}B^ext (r) = \frac{\mu_0 i_s}{2\pi r} e_\theta = \frac{\mu_0 r_w H_0}{r} e_\theta.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-379: The corresponding vector potential outside the metal is \begin{equation}\bm{A}^\text{ext} (r) = \frac{\mu_0 i_s}{2\pi} \ln{ \frac{\sqrt{l^2 + r^2} + l}{r} } \bm{e}_z.\end{equation}.
elements.paragraph-2411-005: Gauge Invariant Vector Potential inside Superconductor

elements.sent-2411-412: Invoking the correspondence principle yields a canonically quantised description of circuit dynamics over a bosonic Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-413: This approach has been very successful for describing experiments, but implicitly starts from the classical Ginsberg-Landau mean field theory for the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-414: This approach does not impose a spontaneously broken symmetry so that it consistently describes quantised circuits that support superpositions of phases, and the canonical commutation relations between phase and charge are derived from the underlying fermionic commutation properties, rather than imposed.
elements.sent-2411-415: The conventional approach to quantising superconducting circuits \cite{ref:decoherence_SCqubits, ref:circuit_quantization starts with the classical circuit equations of motion given by Kirchoff's laws, which are derived from conservation laws and constitutive relations for the circuit.}
elements.sent-2411-416: For conservative circuits, these equations are the Euler-Lagrange equations, which can be generated from a Lagrangian.
elements.sent-2411-417: The GL order parameter is itself a classical mean field, approximating an underlying microscopic electronic Hamiltonian, and the (re-)quantisation procedure of the classical Euler-Lagrange equations for $\phi_j$, described above, yields the conventional bosonic theory for $\hat{\phi _j$ with the conjugate coordinates being the circuit charges, $\hat{n _j$.}}
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=0.7\columnwidth]{figures/flowchart.pdf} \caption{ The conventional approach to circuit quantisation, along the left pathway, conceptually begins with a microscopic electronic theory of interacting fermions in a metal, which proceeds via a BCS ground-state ansatz to a classical Ginsburg-Landau mean-field theory (GL-MFT) of superconductivity in a metal, which is finally re-quantised to produce a quantised theory of a circuit. The approach described here (right pathway) starts at the same point, but uses the GL-MFT as inspiration to define a low-energy BCS subspace, $\mathcal{H}_{\rm BCS}$ (see \cref{eq:HBCS}) and then directly projects the dynamics onto this subspace, resulting in the effective low-energy circuit Hamiltonian. } \label{fig:flowchart}
elements.sent-2411-418: This standard approach, which is illustrated in \cref{fig:flowchart , is practically satisfactory for modelling the vast majority of experiments, however it is conceptually unfulfilling for several reasons.}
elements.sent-2411-419: In this goal, there is a close analogy in modelling low-dimensional semiconducting systems (such as quantum dots \cite{ref:quantumDot_Milburn or quantum Hall fluids \cite{ref:mesoscopic_QO, ref:mesoscopicChannel_QHE ), in which a microscopic Fermi-liquid theory of a semiconductor is projected onto a low-dimensional subspace spanned by the low-energy bound states of a spatially inhomogeonous, weakly-confining potential landscape, yielding effective descriptions in terms of mass-renormalised, few-electron (or hole) systems \cite{ref:mesoscopic_QO .}}}
elements.sent-2411-420: Secondly, GL mean-field theory (MFT) does not typically include phenomena such as quasiparticles or quantum fluctuations in the gap parameter, $\Delta$, which are otherwise added `by-hand' after re-quantisation \cite{ref:Higgs_dynamics_Floquent, ref:quasiparticles_SCqubits .}
elements.sent-2411-421: Thirdly, when re-quantising GL mean-field theory (MFT), the bosonic canonical commutation relation, $[\hat\phi,\hat n]=i$, is postulated \cite{widom1979,Widom1981,https://doi.org/10.1002/cta.2359 , or see \cite{Pandey_2024 for variations in this approach.}}
elements.sent-2411-422: However, it is not clear that this is consistent with the Stone-von Neumann theorem when the spectrum of $\hat\phi$ has compact support on the interval $[-\pi,\pi)$, and the spectrum of $\hat n$ is $\mathbb{Z}$.
elements.sent-2411-423: In fact, we expect to be able to derive the correct commutation relations directly from the underlying fermionic degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-424: Fourthly, GL mean-field theory assumes spontaneous symmetry breaking of the superconducting order parameter.
elements.sent-2411-425: However in the quantised circuit, we expect coherent quantum superpositions of (relative) phases \cite{Mizel2024 , which is conceptually at odds with the spontaneous realisation of only one amongst the large manifold of allowed phases.}
elements.sent-2411-426: Lastly, the classical GL MFT for the order parameter $\phi$ is indifferent to the support of $\phi$: descriptions in which $\phi$ is compact, $\phi\in [-\pi,\pi)$, and non-compact, $\phi\in\mathbb{R}$, are entirely equivalent at a classical level.
elements.sent-2411-427: In contrast, the quantum descriptions of these two alternatives, or anything in between, are not equivalent to one another \cite{ref:compact-noncompact .}
elements.sent-2411-428: We define a projector, $P$ onto $\mathcal{H _{\rm BCS}$, and use this projector to directly construct the low energy Hamiltonian from the microscopic electronic Hamiltonian, as illustrated in \cref{fig:flowchart .}}
elements.sent-2411-429: We note that our projector-based approach to circuit quantisation has some similarity to early work by \citet{Leggett1966 and \citet{Ambegaokar1982 .}}
elements.sent-2411-430: The underlying analytical approach we take is similar to the early work by Widom et al. \cite{widom1979,Widom1981 and recently by \citet{Mizel2024 , starting with a low-energy vector space defined by the BCS ground states.}}
elements.sent-2411-431: We apply this projector-based approach to re-analyse the conventional superconducting toolbox: capacitors, inductors, and Josephson junctions, and we show that we recover the expected results in the limit of large systems.
elements.sent-2411-432: In what follows, we formalise this approach, and derive a number features of the model, including the emergent, discrete basis for the compact Hilbert subspace $\mathcal{H _{\text{BCS }$, the corresponding dimensionality of the low-energy subspace, the canonical commutation relations, and we identify the relevant low-energy Hilbert space of standard lumped-element superconducting components.}}
elements.sent-2411-433: We specifically address the difference between the effective Hilbert space for inductors and Josephson junctions.
elements.sent-2411-434: With this approach, we describe the connection between the microscopic BCS theory of superconductors \cite{ref:BCS_superconductivity_theory and the mesoscopic electronic description of a Cooper-pair-box (CPB) or transmon \cite{ref:CPB , as well as devices that include an inductive element.}}
elements.sent-2411-435: This paper is organised as follows: We firstly compute the overlap of BCS states and discuss the requirements for a Hilbert space that is approximately spanned by them in Section~\ref{sec:LowEspace .}
elements.sent-2411-436: In Section~\ref{sec:LCJprojection , we project a microscopic description of an inductor-capacitor-junction (LCJ) circuit onto the low energy subspace, showing consistency with the conventional result.}
elements.sent-2411-437: Finally we discuss some implications that follow from this analysis, and conclude with potential directions for further work.
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=0.49\columnwidth]{figures/LCJcircuit.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.49\columnwidth]{figures/system.pdf} \caption{ (a) A standard LCJ circuit consists of a inductor $L$, a capacitor $C$, and a Josephson junction $E_J$ made by superconductors. (b) A cartoon of the microscopic elements of a Josephson junction, consisting of two superconducting islands (blue) separated by an insulating tunnel barrier (yellow). At low temperature, the electrons bind into Cooper pairs which tunnel coherently across the (green arrow). In addition, the charges inside the two superconducting islands have a capacitive Coulomb interaction (red arrow). } \label{fig:system}
elements.sent-2411-438: To introduce notation, we consider two pieces of superconducting metal separated by an insulator, which is corresponds to the capacitor and Josephson junction parts of the LCJ circuit shown in \cref{fig:system .}
elements.sent-2411-439: For the Josephson junction illustrated in \cref{fig:system b, the superconducting islands $A$ and $B$ are described by their respecitve Hamiltonians \cite{ref:annett , \begin{equation}\label{eq:Hamiltonian_SCwoMFA} H_{A,B} = H_{K_{A,B}}+H_{I_{A,B}},\end{equation} where the microscopic electron kinetic and interaction terms are given respectively in the momentum basis as \begin{equation}\label{eq:HKA} H_{K_A} = \sum_{k_A \sigma_A} \left( \epsilon_{k_A} - \mu_A \right) c_{k_A \sigma_A}^\dagger c_{k_A \sigma_A}, H_{I_A} = - \abs{g}^2 \sum_{k_Ak_A'} c_{k_A\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-k_A\downarrow}^\dagger c_{-k_A'\downarrow} c_{k_A'\uparrow},\end{equation} and similarly for $H_B$, where $\epsilon_{\bm{k }$ is the single-particle kinetic energy of an electron with momentum $\bm{k}$, $\mu$ is the chemical potential, $\sigma$ labels the spin index, $g$ is the effective coupling strength between electrons, and $c_{x ^\dagger$ and $c_{x}$ are creation and annihilation operators for electron mode $x$.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-160: \begin{equation}\label{eq:HKA} H_{K_A} = \sum_{k_A \sigma_A} \left( \epsilon_{k_A} - \mu_A \right) c_{k_A \sigma_A}^\dagger c_{k_A \sigma_A}, H_{I_A} = - \abs{g}^2 \sum_{k_Ak_A'} c_{k_A\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-k_A\downarrow}^\dagger c_{-k_A'\downarrow} c_{k_A'\uparrow},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-440: Assuming point-like metallic islands, the Coulomb interaction between electrons on island $A$ and $B$ is given by \begin{equation}H_Coul = \lambda_C \sum_{k_A \sigma_A} c_{k_A \sigma_A}^\dagger c_{k_A \sigma_A} \sum_{ k_B \sigma_B}\nonumber c_{k_B \sigma_B}^\dagger c_{k_B \sigma_B}, =4\lambda_C \hat{N}^{\rm CP}_A\hat{N}^{\rm CP}_B\label{eq:Hamiltonian_coulomb}\end{equation} where \mbox{$ \hat{N ^{\rm CP _A = \sum_{\bm{k _A\sigma_A c_{\bm{k _A\sigma_A ^{\dagger c_{\bm{k _A\sigma_A /2 $} and $\lambda_C$ is the effective Coulomb interaction strength between the two point-like superconductor.}}}}}}}}}

elements.formula-2411-162: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Hamiltonian_tunneling} H_T = \displaystyle\sum_{k_A k_B \sigma_A \sigma_B} t_{k_{A}k_{B}} c_{k_A \sigma_A}^{\dagger} c_{k_B \sigma_B} + {\rm h.c.},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-442: The microscopic Hamiltonian for an electrical circuit including tunnel junctions is then \begin{equation}H_{\text{LCJ}} = H_A + H_B + H_\text{Coul} + H_T.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-443: Conventionally, the quartic Hamiltonian \cref{eq:Hamiltonian_SCwoMFA describing each metallic island is treated approximately by factoring into a quadratic Hamiltonian with coefficients computed in mean-field theory, yielding \begin{equation}H_{BCS} = \sum_{k s} ( \epsilon_{k}\! - \!\mu ) c_{k s}^\dagger c_{k s}\nonumber - \abs{g}^2 \sum_{kk'} \big(\langle{ c_{k\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-k\downarrow}^\dagger }\rangle c_{-k'\downarrow} c_{k'\uparrow} +{\rm h.c.}\big), \label{eq:Hamiltonian_BCSquadratic} = \sum_{k s} \left( \epsilon_{k} \!-\! \mu \right) c_{k s}^\dagger c_{k s} \!-\! \sum_{k} ( \Delta^* c_{-k\downarrow} c_{k\uparrow} +{\rm h.c.} ).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-164: \begin{equation}H_{BCS} = \sum_{k s} ( \epsilon_{k}\! - \!\mu ) c_{k s}^\dagger c_{k s}\nonumber - \abs{g}^2 \sum_{kk'} \big(\langle{ c_{k\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-k\downarrow}^\dagger }\rangle c_{-k'\downarrow} c_{k'\uparrow} +{\rm h.c.}\big), \label{eq:Hamiltonian_BCSquadratic} = \sum_{k s} \left( \epsilon_{k} \!-\! \mu \right) c_{k s}^\dagger c_{k s} \!-\! \sum_{k} ( \Delta^* c_{-k\downarrow} c_{k\uparrow} +{\rm h.c.} ).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-444: The quadratic Hamiltonian is diagonalised using the Bogoliubov-Valatin transformation to construct the BCS ground state \begin{equation}\ket{\Psi(\Delta)} = \prod_{\bm{k}} \left( u_{\bm{k}}(|\Delta|) + v_{\bm{k}}(|\Delta|) e^{i \phi} c_{\bm{k}\uparrow}^{\dagger} c_{-\bm{k}\downarrow}^{\dagger} \right) \ket{0},\label{eq:BCSGS}\end{equation} where $u_{\bm{k }$, $v_{\bm{k }$ are variational parameters used to minimise the total energy, $\ket{0}$ is the vacuum of the electronic Fock space, and $\phi\equiv\arg(\Delta)\in[-\pi,\pi)$ is an undetermined phase angle \cite{ref:annett, ref:Coleman .}}}
elements.sent-2411-445: The self-consistent mean field theory is found by requiring that the expectation in the definition of $\Delta$ is taken with respect to \cref{eq:BCSGS .}
elements.sent-2411-446: The Bogoliubov coefficients that minimise the self-consistent energy of \cref{eq:Hamiltonian_BCSquadratic are given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:BogoCoeff} u_{k}^2 = \big(1 + ({\epsilon_{k} - \mu})/{E_{k}}\big)/2 and\nonumber v_{k}^2= \big(1 - ({\epsilon_{k} - \mu})/{E_{k}}\big)/2,\end{equation} where \mbox{$ E_{\bm{k \equiv \sqrt{(\epsilon_{\bm{k - \mu)^2 + \Delta^2 $} \cite{ref:annett .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-447: We use this standard approach to motivate a choice of low energy basis states, but depart from it in that we do not impose the symmetry breaking implied by taking the partial expectations that transforms the microscopic quartic Hamiltonian in \cref{eq:Hamiltonian_SCwoMFA into the quadratic form in \cref{eq:Hamiltonian_BCSquadratic .}}
elements.sent-2411-448: In this section, we use the \mbox{phase-symmetry-broken BCS ground state $\ket{\Psi(\Delta)}$ to motivate the introduction of a low-energy Hilbert space spanned by a set of linearly independent BCS ground states $\{\ket{\Psi(\phi)}\}_{\phi\in[-\pi,\pi)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-449: To understand the structure of this Hilbert space, we analyse the overlap between BCS states from this set, and determine conditions under which we can construct an approximately orthogonal and complete basis.
elements.sent-2411-450: We then construct ground-space phase and number operators, and connect the resulting phase-number commutator to the well-known Pegg-Barnett theory \cite{ref:Pegg Barnett .}
elements.sent-2411-451: Motivated by \cref{eq:BCSGS , we define a family of BCS-type ground state wavefunctions for a superconducting island as \cite{ref:BCS_superconductivity_theory,Mizel2024 \begin{equation}\label{eq:BCS_state} \ket{\Psi(\phi_j)} = {\prod}_{\bm{k}} \left( u_{\bm{k}} + v_{\bm{k}} e^{i\phi_j} c_{\bm{k}\uparrow}^{\dagger} c_{-\bm{k}\downarrow}^{\dagger} \right) \ket{0},\end{equation} where $\phi_j\in [-\pi,\pi)$ parameterises the allowed set of BCS states.}}
elements.sent-2411-452: We adopt the subscript $j$ to preempt our intent to restrict the allowed phases to a discrete subset \mbox{$\mathcal{Z =\{\phi_j\}_{j\in \Zjmax}$} where $\jmax$ is the effective dimension of the Hilbert space, which we will compute below.}
elements.sent-2411-453: The core of our approach here is to define a basis of BCS ground states $\{\ket{\Psi(\phi_j)}\}_{\phi_j\in \mathcal{Z }$ and the corresponding low-energy Hilbert space \begin{equation}\mathcal{H}_{\rm BCS}=\text{span}\{\ket{\Psi(\phi_j)}\}_{\phi_j\in \mathcal{Z}}.\label{eq:HBCS}\end{equation} Projecting the microscopic electronic Hamiltonian into this subspace allows us to construct the low-energy circuit theory without passing through the conventional GL MFT followed by a re-quantisation procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-454: For a system consisting of multiple superconducting islands, the total Hilbert space is formed from a tensor product of the island subsystems.
elements.sent-2411-456: This work is focused on the dynamics of an electronic system restricted to the BCS ground space, so we define an (approximate) BCS ground space projection operator $P$ onto $\mathcal{H _{\text{BCS }$ \cite{ref:POM as \begin{equation}\label{eq:projector} P = \sum_j \dyad{\Psi(\phi_j)},\end{equation} with the complementary projector defined as \mbox{$\bar{P =\mathbb{I - P$}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-457: The degree to which this is a well-defined, idempotent projector satisfying $P^2=P$ depends on the orthogonality of the low-energy phase basis, which we address below.
elements.sent-2411-458: For example, in a system of two superconducting islands with a microscopic electronic Hamiltonian consisting of intra-island, and inter-island Coulomb and tunnelling terms, \begin{equation}H_{\rm el}=H_A + H_B + H_\text{Coul}+H_T,\end{equation} the low-energy effective Hamiltonian is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:Heff} PH_{eff}P \nonumber \approx PH_0P + PH_1P \quad+ PH_1\bar{P} \left( \bar{P}E_0 - \bar{P}H_0\bar{P} \right)^{-1} \bar{P}H_1P,\end{equation} up to the second-order in the tunneling, where \mbox{$H_0 = H_A + H_B + H_\text{Coul}$} and $H_1 = H_T$ is treated to second order in perturbation theory \cite{ref:POM,Ambegaokar1982 .}

elements.formula-2411-179: \begin{equation}\label{eq:completeness_distance} d(\ket{\Psi(\phi)}, H_{\rm BCS}), \nonumber = \underset{\{a_j\}}{\min}\; d\big( \ket{\Psi(\phi)}, {\sum}_j a_j \ket{\Psi(\phi_j)} \big), \\\nonumber = \underset{\{a_j\}}{\min}\; \Big|{ \ket{\Psi(\phi)} - {\sum}_j a_j \ket{\Psi(\phi_j)} }\Big|, \\\nonumber \leq \underset{\{a_j\}}{\min}\; \sqrt{2\big( 1 - {\sum}_j a_j \,|W (\phi_j - \phi)| \big)}, \\\nonumber \leq \sqrt{2 \min_j(1- |W (\phi_j - \phi)| )}, \leq \sqrt{2(1-|W ( \delta \phi)|)},\nonumber \approx \sqrt{2(1-e^{-\pi \alpha^2/(\ycl{2\alpha_0^2})})}.\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-534: Having constructed a low energy Hilbert space from a discrete basis of phase states, in this section we define a basis of ``number'' states through the Fourier transform.

elements.sent-2411-537: Here we define low-energy ``number'' eigenstates $\ket{\Psi(N)}$ via a discrete Fourier transform \begin{equation}\label{eq:|N>_def} \ket{\Psi(N)} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\jmax}} \sum_{\phi_j\inZ} e^{-iN\phi_j} \ket{\Psi(\phi_j)}\end{equation} We show in \cref{append:N_eigenstate that $\ket{\Psi(N)}$ are eigenstates of the microscopic Cooper pair number operator \mbox{$ \hat{N ^{\rm CP = \sum_{\bm{k}\sigma c_{\bm{k}\sigma ^{\dagger c_{\bm{k}\sigma /2 $}, i.e. \begin{equation}\label{eq:|N>_eigenEq} \hat{N}^{\rm CP} \ket{\Psi(N)} = N \ket{\Psi(N)},\end{equation} establishing that the Fourier conjugate states are indeed charge-number eigenstates, and the set $\{\ket{\Psi(N)}\}$ defines a conjugate charge-number basis with \mbox{$N\in\{\frac{n {2 - \frac{\jmax {2 ,..., \frac{n {2 + \frac{\jmax {2}\}\subset\mathbb{Z}$}.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-585: Here we analyse the inductance of a superconducting wire.
elements.sent-2411-586: The inductive energy arises from two contributions: the kinetic energy of the electrons in motion, and the potential energy in the magnetic field induced by the current.
elements.sent-2411-587: Both terms contribute to the effective nodal flux, $\Phi$, at each point in a circuit, which is itself determined by the superconducting phase, $\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-588: We consider a superconducting wire with finite cross-section area shown in \cref{fig:SC_straightWire (a).}
elements.sent-2411-589: For notational simplicity, we will take the wire to be cylindrical, so that the problem is a 2D system (while realistic microelectronic wires are 3D), and the superconducting phase and the vector potential depend on both the axial and radial coordinates, $z$ and $r$.
elements.sent-2411-590: Our goal is to derive the relevant Hilbert space and the constitutive relation for a lumped inductive element from the microscopic wavefunction of a distributed, spatially-varying phase in a superconducting wire of length $l$.
elements.sent-2411-591: We start with a brief review of the classical analysis of a superconducting inductive wire, with more detail given in \cref{append:Afield_SCwire .}
elements.sent-2411-592: Classically, the current density, $\bm{J (\bm{r )$, in a superconductor is related to the vector potential, $\bm{A ^\text{int (\bm{r )$, \cite{ref:Jackson_electrodynamics \begin{equation}\label{eq:JA} \bm{J} (\bm{r}) = \dfrac{1}{\mu_0} \curl{\curl{\bm{A}^\text{int} (\bm{r})}},\end{equation} where $\bm{A ^\text{int (\bm{r )$ is the vector potential on the interior of the wire, and $\bm{J (\bm{r ) = J_z (r) \bm{e _z$ is axially symmetric.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-593: For convenience we choose a gauge in which the vector potential is also axially symmetric, so that \begin{equation}\label{eq:Agauge} \bm{A} (\bm{r}) = A_z(r) \bm{e}_z,\end{equation} and we give more details about the form of $A_z(r)$ in \cref{eq:Afield_int .}
elements.sent-2411-594: In an axially symmetric gauge, we solve \cref{eq:JA to find \begin{equation}A^\text{int}_z(r) = -\mu_0\int_0^r dr' \,r'\ln(r/r')J_z(r').\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-595: That is, $A^\text{int _z$ is a linear functional of the current density.}

elements.sent-2411-692: We have described here a direct approach to the quantisation of superconducting circuits.
elements.sent-2411-693: By projecting the microscopic Hamiltonian onto the low-energy BCS subspace, we derive the effective Hamiltonian of a superconducting LCJ circuit from first principles.
elements.sent-2411-694: With this fully-quantum framework, we establish a direct pathway from the microscopic description to the macroscopic picture of a quantised circuit.
elements.sent-2411-695: The low-energy BCS space has a natural discretisation that emerges from the structure of the BCS overlap function.
elements.sent-2411-696: We have shown how to construct phase and charge operators on this subspace, and derived their commutation relation.
elements.sent-2411-697: The projective approach we have described here reproduces the known results for capacitive, inductive and Josephson tunnelling elements.
elements.sent-2411-698: One conclusion from our analysis is that since inductors are inherently extended objects, the Hilbert space associated to them is larger than that for point-like capacitive and Josephson tunnelling elements, whose Hilbert space is periodic on the interval $[-\pi ,\pi)$.
elements.sent-2411-699: We believe that this resolves some of the questions about the compactness or otherwise of superconducting circuits reviewed in \cite{ref:compact-noncompact .}
elements.sent-2411-700: The approach we take here opens a number of directions for further work.
elements.sent-2411-701: Firstly, we have given a physically-motivated argument to estimate the support of the accessible Hilbert space of an inductive element, and we postulate that this will emerge dynamically from decoherence induced by quasi-particle formation at sufficiently high currents.
elements.sent-2411-702: This could be formalised by extending the projective subspace to include few-quasiparticle states.
elements.sent-2411-703: These additional modes will produce decoherence relative to the BCS subspace, and should dynamically constrain the accessible states of extended wire-like inductive elements.
elements.sent-2411-704: Secondly, while there has been progress since \citet{Widom1981 in developing the theory of quantum-phase slip (QPS) devices (e.g.\ see \cite{ref:2nlinearSCqubit ), to our knowledge there is as yet no microscopic derivation analogous to that for Josephson junctions.}}
elements.sent-2411-705: We expect that a detailed projective analysis of superconducting wires whose thickness is comparable to or smaller than the penetration depth provides a viable pathway to realise such a derivation.
elements.sent-2411-706: Lastly, while we have focussed on the superconducting phase degree of freedom, $\phi$, the BCS family of states admits the possibility of quantising the gap amplitude, $|\Delta|$, which should yield Higgs-like excitations of superconducting islands.
elements.sent-2411-707: We have provided a starting point for this analysis, and speculate that this may provide for a greatly expanded family of superconducting quantum devices that could be built experimentally.
elements.sent-2411-708: TMS and YCL acknowledge funding from the Australian Research Council Centre of Excellence for Engineered Quantum Systems (Project No.\ CE170100009).. \end{acknowledgments \bibliographystyle{apsrev4-2 .}}

elements.sent-2411-721: The propagator $U_0(\tau)$ can be expanded as the time ordered series \begin{equation}U_0(\tau) = T_{\tau} \exp( \dfrac{-i}{\hbar} \int_0^\tau \dd{t} H_0(t) ), = T_{\tau} \left( I - \dfrac{i}{\hbar} \int_0^\tau \dd{t} H_0(t) + \cdots \right),\end{equation} such that \begin{equation}U_0(\tau) H_T(\tau) H_TD_{\varphi} = T_{\tau} \big( H_T(\tau) H_TD_{\varphi} \quad- \dfrac{i}{\hbar} \int_0^\tau \dd{t} H_0(t) H_T(\tau) H_TD_{\varphi} \quad+ \cdots \big), \approx T_{\tau} \left( H_T(\tau) H_TD_{\varphi} \right).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-735: Consider a superconductor volume ``SC" in the presence of a vector potential $\bm{A}$.
elements.sent-2411-736: The Hamiltonian is given by \cite{ref:Gorkov, ref:Coleman \begin{equation}\label{eq:Hamiltonian_SCspatial} H_{K_A} + H_{I_A} \nonumber = \int_SC \dd{V} \big\{ c_{rs}^\dagger \big[ \tfrac{1}{2m} ( p - e A^int )^2 - \mu \big] c_{rs} \quad- \abs{g}^2 c_{r\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-r\downarrow}^\dagger c_{-r'\downarrow} c_{r'\uparrow} \big\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-737: In the Hamiltonian, the electron momentum operator $\bm{p}$ acts on the fermionic Hilbert space, while the vector potential operator $\bm{A ^\text{int}$ acts on the electromagnetic Hilbert space.}
elements.sent-2411-738: However, in the low-frequency limit, the $\bm{A ^\text{int}$ field will be determined by the local current density, according to London's equation.}
elements.sent-2411-739: The fermionic field operators in the spatial representation are the Fourier transform of those in the momentum space \cite{ref:Coleman , \begin{equation}\label{eq:FourierTrans_fieldOPelectron} c_{\bm{r}s} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{V}} \sum_{\bm{k}} e^{i\bm{k} \cdot \bm{r}} c_{\bm{k}s}.\end{equation}.}

elements.formula-2411-256: \begin{equation}\ket{\Psi(\phi)} \nonumber = \prod_{k} \left( u_{k} + v_{k} e^{i\phi} c_{k\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-k\downarrow}^\dagger \right) \ket{0}, \\\nonumber = \prod_{k} \left( u_{k} + v_{k} e^{i\phi} \int \dd{r_{k}} \int \dd{r'_{k}} e^{i k \cdot (r_{k} - r'_{k})} c_{r_{k}\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-r'_{k}\downarrow}^\dagger \right) \ket{0}, \\\nonumber = \sum_{m=0}^n \sum_{\chi\inS_m} \prod_{k_i\in\chi^c} u_{k_i} \prod_{k_j\in\chi} v_{k_j} e^{i\phi} \int \dd{r_{k_j}} \int \dd{r'_{k_j}} e^{i k_j \cdot (r_{k_j} - r'_{k_j})} c_{r_{k_j}\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-r'_{k_j}\downarrow}^\dagger \ket{0}, \\\nonumber = \sum_{m=0}^n \sum_{\chi\inS_m} \prod_{k_i\in\chi^c} u_{k_i} \prod_{k_j\in\chi} v_{k_j} \int \dd{r_{k_j}} \int \dd{r'_{k_j}} e^{i k_j \cdot (r_{k_j} - r'_{k_j})} \left( e^{i\phi/2} c_{r_{k_j}\uparrow}^\dagger \right) \left( e^{i\phi/2} c_{-r'_{k_j}\downarrow}^\dagger \right) \ket{0}, \rightarrow \sum_{m=0}^n \sum_{\chi\inS_m} \prod_{k_i\in\chi^c} u_{k_i} \prod_{k_j\in\chi} v_{k_j} \int \dd{r_{k_j}} \int \dd{r'_{k_j}} e^{i k_j \cdot (r_{k_j} - r'_{k_j})} \left( e^{i\phi(r)/2} c_{r_{k_j}\uparrow}^\dagger \right) \left( e^{i\phi(r)/2} c_{-r'_{k_j}\downarrow}^\dagger \right) \ket{0}, \label{eq:BCS_spatial_phase} = \prod_{k} \left[ u_{k} + v_{k} \left( e^{i\phi(r)/2} c_{k\uparrow}^\dagger \right) \left( e^{i\phi(r)/2} c_{-k\downarrow}^\dagger \right) \right] \ket{0}, \\\nonumber \equiv \ket{\Psi[\phi(r)]}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-749: In this appendix, we solve the London equation for a superconducting wire in cylindrical coordinates and compute the magnetic field inside the wire.
elements.sent-2411-750: We then integrate the flux around the wire out to a cutoff distance, and calculate the geometric inductance as the magnetic field is linked to the current.
elements.paragraph-2411-010: Gauge Invariant Vector Potential inside a Superconductor
elements.sent-2411-751: We obtain the gauge invariant vector potential inside the superconductor: \begin{equation}\tilde{\bm{A}}^{\text{int}} (r) = \bm{A}^{\text{int}} (r) + \dfrac{\Phi_0}{2\pi} \grad{\hat{\phi}},\end{equation} where the scalar $\phi$-field is self-generated by the superconductor.
elements.sent-2411-752: We can obtain $L_G$ by integrating the flux outside the wire, induced by the current $\Phi_\text{ext}$, which is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:flux_induced} \Phi_\text{ext} = l \int_{r_w}^R \dd{r} \abs{\bm{B}^{\rm ext} (r)} = \underbrace{ \dfrac{\mu_0 l \ln(R/r_w)}{2\pi} }_{=L_G} \cdot i_s.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-753: By placing a superconductor in an magnetic field, the field penetrates the metal to a small depth, called the London penetration depth $\lambda_L$.
elements.sent-2411-754: The electrons within this region move to induce the Meissner effect, and giving rise to a kinetic inductance.
elements.sent-2411-755: Given the London penetration depth $\lambda_L = \sqrt{m/(\mu_0 De^2)}$ and the effective cross-section area $\sigma_{\text{eff \approx 2\pi r_w \lambda_L$, the $\Phi_\text{int}$ can be written as \begin{equation}\label{eq:flux_internal2D} \Phi_\text{int} = i_s \cdot \underbrace{ \left( \dfrac{m}{De^2} \dfrac{l}{\sigma_{\text{eff}}} \right) }_{\equiv L_K}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-756: Approximating the superconducting wire to a point-like wire, the internal magnetic field is given by $ \bm{B ^{\text{int = \bm{B ^{\text{int (r=r_w) = \frac{\mu_0 i_s {2\pi r_w \bm{e _\theta, $ so the flux enclosed reads \begin{equation}\label{eq:flux_internal1D} \Phi_\text{int} = \int_{\bm{\sigma}_{\text{eff}}} \bm{B}^{\text{int}} \vdot \dd{\bm{\sigma}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 i_s l \lambda_L}{2\pi r_w} = L_K i_s.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}
